title: Chapter 2: JSON APIs And Ajax - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of JSON, fetch API, XMLHttpRequest, and handling API data

questions.question: What does JSON stand for?
questions.explanation: JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation. It is a lightweight data interchange format that is easy for humans to read and write and easy for machines to parse and generate. Despite the name, JSON is language-independent and used widely across programming languages.

questions.question: Which method converts a JSON string into a JavaScript object?
questions.explanation: JSON.parse() takes a JSON string and converts it into a JavaScript object or value. JSON.stringify() does the opposite — it converts a JavaScript object into a JSON string. These are the two core JSON methods in JavaScript.

questions.question: What does the fetch() function return?

options.text: The response data directly as a JavaScript object
A Promise that resolves to a Response object
A callback function containing the response

questions.explanation: fetch() returns a Promise that resolves to a Response object. You then call .json(), .text(), or other methods on the Response to extract the body content. For example: fetch(url).then(response => response.json()).then(data => console.log(data)).

questions.question: In an XMLHttpRequest, which readyState value indicates that the request is complete and the response is ready?
questions.explanation: XMLHttpRequest readyState 4 means DONE — the operation is complete. The states are: 0 (UNSENT), 1 (OPENED), 2 (HEADERS_RECEIVED), 3 (LOADING), and 4 (DONE). You typically check for readyState === 4 && status === 200 in the onreadystatechange handler.

questions.question: Which method on a fetch Response object parses the response body as JSON?
questions.explanation: response.json() reads the response body and parses it as JSON, returning a Promise that resolves with the parsed data. Other body-reading methods include .text(), .blob(), .formData(), and .arrayBuffer(). Each can only be called once since the body stream is consumed.

questions.question: What does AJAX stand for?
questions.explanation: AJAX stands for Asynchronous JavaScript And XML. It refers to the technique of making HTTP requests from the browser without reloading the page. Despite the name, modern AJAX typically uses JSON instead of XML, and fetch() instead of XMLHttpRequest.

questions.question: Which JavaScript method is used to access the user's geographic location in the browser?
questions.explanation: navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition(successCallback, errorCallback) requests the user's current position. The browser prompts the user for permission. On success, the callback receives a Position object with coordinates (latitude, longitude, etc.).

questions.question: Which event handler on XMLHttpRequest is called when the readyState changes?
questions.explanation: The onreadystatechange event handler is called whenever the readyState property changes. It fires multiple times during a request (states 1-4). Inside this handler, you typically check if readyState === 4 and status === 200 before processing the response.

questions.question: How do you handle errors when using the fetch API with Promises?

options.text: Chaining a .catch() method to the Promise chain

questions.explanation: With Promises, errors are handled by chaining .catch() at the end of the Promise chain: fetch(url).then(res => res.json()).then(data => ...).catch(err => ...). Note that fetch only rejects on network failures — HTTP error status codes (like 404) still resolve and must be checked via response.ok.

questions.question: Which property of the document object allows you to add a click event listener in vanilla JavaScript?
questions.explanation: document.addEventListener('click', callback) is the standard way to add event listeners in modern JavaScript. It allows multiple listeners for the same event and works on any DOM element. attachEvent() was an older IE-specific method that is no longer used.
